Question: What are two methods used to graph parametric equations?
Answer: plotting points with the orientation arrow and a graphing calculator

Question: What is one difference in point-plotting parametric equations compared to Cartesian equations?
Why are some graphs drawn with arrows?
Answer: The arrows show the orientation, the direction of motion according to increasing values of $$t .$$

Question: Name a few common types of graphs of parametric equations.
Why are parametric graphs important in understanding projectile motion?
Answer: The parametric equations show the different vertical and horizontal motions over time.

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, look at the graphs that were created by parametric equations of the form $$\{ \left .\begin{matrix}{a}x ( t ) = a \text{cos} ( b t ) \\ y ( t ) = c \text{sin} ( d t )\end{matrix}\right . .$$ Use the parametric mode on the graphing calculator to find the values of $$a , b , c ,$$ and $$d$$ to achieve each graph.

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, use a graphing utility to graph the given parametric equations.
Question: Explain the effect on the graph of the parametric equation when we changed the domain.

An object is thrown in the air with vertical velocity of 20 ft/s and horizontal velocity of 15 ft/s. The object’s height can be described by the equation $$y ( t ) = - 16 t^{2} + 20 t$$ , while the object moves horizontally with constant velocity 15 ft/s. Write parametric equations for the object’s position, and then eliminate time to write height as a function of horizontal position.

A skateboarder riding on a level surface at a constant speed of 9 ft/s throws a ball in the air, the height of which can be described by the equation $$y ( t ) = - 16 t^{2} + 10 t + 5 .$$ Write parametric equations for the ball’s position, and then eliminate time to write height as a function of horizontal position.

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, use this scenario: A dart is thrown upward with an initial velocity of 64 ft/s at an angle of elevation of 52°. Consider the position of the dart at any time $$t .$$ Neglect air resistance.
Question: Find parametric equations that model the problem situation.
Answer: $$\{ \left .\begin{matrix}{a}x ( t ) = 64 t \cos{} ( 52 __{degree} ) \\ y ( t ) = - 16 t^{2} + 64 t \sin{} ( 52 __{degree} )\end{matrix}\right .$$

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, use this scenario: A dart is thrown upward with an initial velocity of 64 ft/s at an angle of elevation of 52°. Consider the position of the dart at any time $$t .$$ Neglect air resistance.
Question: Find all possible values of $$x$$ that represent the situation.

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, use this scenario: A dart is thrown upward with an initial velocity of 64 ft/s at an angle of elevation of 52°. Consider the position of the dart at any time $$t .$$ Neglect air resistance.
Question: When will the dart hit the ground?

Question: Find the maximum height of the dart.

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, use this scenario: A dart is thrown upward with an initial velocity of 64 ft/s at an angle of elevation of 52°. Consider the position of the dart at any time $$t .$$ Neglect air resistance.
Question: At what time will the dart reach maximum height?

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, look at the graphs of each of the four parametric equations. Although they look unusual and beautiful, they are so common that they have names, as indicated in each exercise. Use a graphing utility to graph each on the indicated domain.
Question: An epicycloid: $$\{ \left .\begin{matrix}{a}x ( t ) = 14 \cos{} t - \cos{} ( 14 t ) \\ y ( t ) = 14 \sin{} t + \sin{} ( 14 t )\end{matrix}\right .$$ on the domain $$[ 0 , 2 \pi ]$$ .

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, look at the graphs of each of the four parametric equations. Although they look unusual and beautiful, they are so common that they have names, as indicated in each exercise. Use a graphing utility to graph each on the indicated domain.
Question: A hypocycloid: $$\{ \left .\begin{matrix}{a}x ( t ) = 6 \sin{} t + 2 \sin{} ( 6 t ) \\ y ( t ) = 6 \cos{} t - 2 \cos{} ( 6 t )\end{matrix}\right .$$ on the domain $$[ 0 , 2 \pi ]$$ .

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, look at the graphs of each of the four parametric equations. Although they look unusual and beautiful, they are so common that they have names, as indicated in each exercise. Use a graphing utility to graph each on the indicated domain.
Question: A hypotrochoid: $$\{ \left .\begin{matrix}{a}x ( t ) = 2 \sin{} t + 5 \cos{} ( 6 t ) \\ y ( t ) = 5 \cos{} t - 2 \sin{} ( 6 t )\end{matrix}\right .$$ on the domain $$[ 0 , 2 \pi ]$$ .

Problem Statement: For the following exercises, look at the graphs of each of the four parametric equations. Although they look unusual and beautiful, they are so common that they have names, as indicated in each exercise. Use a graphing utility to graph each on the indicated domain.
Question: A rose: $$\{ \left .\begin{matrix}{a}x ( t ) = 5 \sin{} ( 2 t ) \sin{} t \\ y ( t ) = 5 \sin{} ( 2 t ) \cos{} t\end{matrix}\right .$$ on the domain $$[ 0 , 2 \pi ]$$ .
